ontology.foodex2_description: Filled pastry, deep-fried — curry puff type
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for curry puff exists; A0CKN (filled pastry, fried) is the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for curry puff; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium-sized curry puff weighing approximately 65 g is a typical single-serve portion sold at hawker stalls and bakeries across Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Chicken) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values are accepted as exact match from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat (9.0 g/100g) is estimated from the HPB fat total using standard deep-fried pastry fatty acid ratios (approximately 41% saturated of total fat) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for curry puff / fried filled pastry (USDA SR Legacy #21052 analogue); this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Curry Puff — analogous estimate based on deep-fried filled wheat pastry with potato filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken curry puff exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: deep-fried wheat pastry with starchy potato filling, consistent with published GI values for similar fried pastry products (GI range 65–72) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (68 × 16.01) / 100 ≈ 10.9, where carbohydrate per 65 g serving = 24.63 × 0.65 = 16.01 g. Input record GL of 16.7 appears to have been calculated on a different serving size basis; the recalculated value of 10.9 per 65 g serving is used here.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 428 mg/100g with minimal potassium offset and high saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit curry puff to one piece occasionally, avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, and prefer baked versions to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-GI (68) refined wheat pastry with potato filling yields a glycaemic load of approximately 10.9 per serving, with only 2.27 g/100g dietary fibre to blunt postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict curry puff to one small piece per occasion, pair it with a high-fibre vegetable side dish to moderate the glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (21.78 g/100g) with saturated fat approximately 9.0 g/100g and trans fat approximately 0.2 g/100g from pastry shortening, combined with 428 mg/100g sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat curry puff as an occasional indulgence only, choosing baked over deep-fried preparations where available, and limiting portion to one piece to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~9.0 g/100g) exceeds 5 g/100g threshold; trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from shortening is directly pro-inflammatory; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit curry puff due to its pro-inflammatory fat profile, AGE content from deep-frying, and nightshade potato filling that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry puff (chicken) is a deep-fried savoury pastry that is one of Singapore's most iconic hawker and bakery snacks, with strong roots in Mamak (Indian-Muslim) culinary tradition. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour dough enriched with shortening, which is folded and crimped to create a flaky, golden-brown crust. The filling consists of diced chicken, cubed potato, hard-boiled egg slices, and a fragrant curry paste mixture seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli. The finished product is deep-fried until crisp and served hot. Per 100 g, curry puff provides 329 kcal, 8.56 g protein, 21.78 g fat (of which approximately 9.0 g is saturated and 0.2 g is trans fat from shortening), 24.63 g carbohydrate, 3.1 g sugar, 2.27 g dietary fibre, and 428 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 68 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 10.9 per 65 g serving. Clinically, curry puff is a high-concern food for hypertension (high sodium, high saturated fat), Type 2 Diabetes (medium-GI refined pastry with starchy potato filling and low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated and trans fat, high sodium). It is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and is widely available at hawker centres, kopitiams, and bakeries throughout Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a curry puff?
What is the GI of curry puff?
How much fat is in a curry puff?
Is curry puff suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact match. Saturated fat adapted from HPB total fat using standard deep-fried pastry fatty acid ratios cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for similar fried wheat pastry with potato filling. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singaporean dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this traditional Singaporean deep-fried pastry preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g of 0.2 g/100g is an estimate based on the presence of shortening in the pastry shell, consistent with the input health_suitability notes; this is not directly reported by HPB and should be confirmed by laboratory analysis. 3. saturated_fat_g of 9.0 g/100g is adapted from HPB total fat (21.78 g/100g) using a 41% saturated fraction typical of deep-fried shortening-based pastry, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy analogue data. 4. Glycaemic load per serving recalculated at 10.9 for a 65 g serving (24.63 g carbohydrate × 0.65 × GI 68 / 100); the input record GL of 16.7 appears to reflect a different serving size assumption. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CKN is used as the closest defensible analogue for filled fried pastry; no dedicated curry puff code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. 6. No SNOMED CT code identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.
